sections.title: L7 — Ecosystem
sections.content:
### L7: Beer Manufacturing Ecosystem

The complete ecosystem of interdependent breweries, equipment OEMs, ingredient suppliers, packaging companies, distributors, regulators (TTB, FDA, HACCP), and retailers coordinated around the production and sale of beer and malt beverages. NAICS 312120 comprises ~5,000 active U.S. establishments (~62,000 employees). The global brewery equipment market reached ~$18.5B in 2024 with a CAGR of 5–6% driven by craft brewery expansion, automation adoption, and sustainability mandates. [SRC: web_search, Census Bureau]

---

sections.title: L6 — System-of-Systems Decomposition
sections.content:
Five L6 core process steps follow the physical transformation sequence from grain to packaged beer. Four L6 horizontal steps run in parallel across all core steps.

| L6 ID | Name | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L6.1** | Grain Handling & Milling | Core | Receive, store, and crush malt and adjuncts into grist for mashing. |
| **L6.2** | Brewhouse | Core | Mash grist with hot water, separate wort, boil with hops, whirlpool, and cool — the core sugar extraction and flavour development sequence. |
| **L6.3** | Fermentation & Conditioning | Core | Pitch yeast into cooled wort; convert sugars to alcohol and CO2; mature beer to flavour target. |
| **L6.4** | Bright Beer Processing | Core | Filter, carbonate, and stabilize finished beer for packaging. |
| **L6.5** | Packaging | Core | Fill beer into bottles, cans, kegs, or bulk containers for distribution. |
| **L6.6** | Glycol Cooling & Refrigeration (H) | Horizontal | Central glycol/water chiller system distributing coolant to fermenters, bright tanks, and cold rooms. **Marquardt primary position.** |
| **L6.7** | Clean-In-Place & Sanitation (H) | Horizontal | Automated cleaning of all vessels, lines, and heat exchangers without disassembly. **Marquardt secondary position.** |
| **L6.8** | Water Treatment & Liquor Supply (H) | Horizontal | Treat, store, and distribute brewing water ("liquor") at required temperatures. **Marquardt tertiary position.** |
| **L6.9** | Utilities, CO2 & Quality Assurance (H) | Horizontal | Steam generation, compressed air, CO2 recovery, wastewater treatment, and laboratory QA. |

---

sections.title: L6.1 — Grain Handling & Milling
sections.content:
First step in the transformation chain. Malt quality and grist consistency directly determine mashing efficiency and wort yield.

### L5 Systems under L6.1

#### L5.1.1: Grain Silo & Storage

Bulk storage of base malt, specialty malts, and adjuncts (rice, corn, wheat). Temperature and moisture monitoring prevents spoilage.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.1.1: Silo Vessel | Stainless steel or galvanized vertical silo; 5–100 t capacity |
| L4.1.1.2: Conveying & Transfer | Bucket elevator, screw conveyor, or pneumatic transport from silo to mill |
| L4.1.1.3: Moisture & Temperature Monitor | Capacitive moisture probe and thermocouple for grain condition tracking |

#### L5.1.2: Malt Mill

Crushes grain into grist, exposing starch granules for enzyme access during mashing. Roller gap setting is critical — too fine clogs the lauter; too coarse reduces yield.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.2.1: Roller Assembly | Two-roller or four-roller mill; gap adjustable 0.7–1.5 mm |
| L4.1.2.2: Mill Drive Motor | Electric motor 2–15 kW depending on throughput |
| L4.1.2.3: Grist Hopper & Dust Extraction | Collection hopper below rollers; aspiration system for grain dust (explosion safety) |

---

sections.title: L6.2 — Brewhouse
sections.content:
The heart of the brewery. Four sequential sub-operations — mashing, lautering, boiling, and whirlpool — extract fermentable sugars from grain, develop flavour with hops, and produce clear, sterile wort. Wort cooling completes the brewhouse cycle.

### L5 Systems under L6.2

#### L5.2.1: Mash Tun / Mash Mixer

Vessel where grist is mixed with hot water ("liquor") at controlled temperatures (62–72 °C) to activate enzymes that convert starch to fermentable sugars. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.1.1: Vessel & False Bottom | Insulated stainless vessel with perforated false bottom or mash screen |
| L4.2.1.2: Agitator / Rake | Variable-speed rake or paddle maintaining uniform temperature and preventing dough balls |
| L4.2.1.3: Temperature Sensor & Steam Jacket | Pt100/NTC sensor + steam or electric jacket for step-mash temperature control |

#### L5.2.2: Lauter Tun

Separates sweet wort from spent grain. The grain bed acts as a natural filter. Sparging (rinsing) extracts residual sugars. Lauter efficiency determines brewhouse yield (typically 75–85%). [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.2.1: Lauter Vessel & Screen | Vessel with slotted false bottom; diameter-to-depth ratio optimized for grain bed depth |
| L4.2.2.2: Raking Mechanism | Motorized knives cutting channels in grain bed to maintain runoff rate |
| L4.2.2.3: Sparge Arm & Flow Control | Rotating sparge arm distributing hot water over grain bed; flow rate controls extract efficiency |

#### L5.2.3: Brew Kettle

Boils wort for 60–90 minutes. Purposes: hop isomerization (bitterness), protein coagulation (hot break), sterilization, and dimethyl sulfide (DMS) drive-off. Energy-intensive step (~35% of total brewery energy). [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.3.1: Kettle Vessel & Heating System | Stainless vessel with internal calandria, external boiler, or direct-fire burner |
| L4.2.3.2: Vapour Condenser / Energy Recovery | Captures steam for hot-water pre-heating; reduces energy consumption 15–20% |
| L4.2.3.3: Hop Dosing System | Manual or automated hop addition at timed intervals (bittering, flavour, aroma) |

#### L5.2.4: Whirlpool

Separates trub (coagulated protein, hop debris) from clear wort using centrifugal settling. Tangential inlet creates vortex; solids collect in a cone at the centre. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.4.1: Whirlpool Vessel | Flat-bottomed stainless vessel; may be combined with kettle in small breweries |
| L4.2.4.2: Tangential Inlet Nozzle | High-velocity inlet creating rotational flow; positioning determines separation efficiency |
| L4.2.4.3: Trub Collection & Drain | Centre drain and trub discharge valve; trub sent to spent grain handling |

#### L5.2.5: Wort Heat Exchanger

Rapidly cools wort from ~100 °C to fermentation temperature (10–20 °C) in minutes. Speed prevents contamination and DMS formation. Counter-flow plate heat exchanger is standard; cold liquor from CLT absorbs heat and is recovered as hot liquor in the HLT. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.5.1: Plate Heat Exchanger | Gasketed or brazed stainless plate pack; 2-stage (water + glycol) in larger breweries |
| L4.2.5.2: Cold Liquor Supply Valve | Modulating valve controlling cold water flow to match wort outlet temperature setpoint |
| L4.2.5.3: Hot Liquor Recovery Valve | Diverts heated water to HLT for energy recovery; saves 10–20% of heating energy |

---

sections.title: L6.3 — Fermentation & Conditioning
sections.content:
The biochemical core of brewing. Yeast converts wort sugars (maltose, glucose) to ethanol, CO2, and flavour compounds. Fermentation temperature control is critical: ales 15–22 °C, lagers 8–14 °C. Conditioning (lagering) matures flavour over days to weeks.

### L5 Systems under L6.3

#### L5.3.1: Conical Fermenter / Unitank

Primary fermentation vessel. Cylindro-conical design (CCT) dominates — cone bottom allows yeast harvesting and CIP without transfer. Unitanks combine fermentation and conditioning in one vessel. Glycol-jacketed for precise temperature control (±0.5 °C). [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.1.1: Stainless Vessel & Cone | 304 or 316L stainless; 60° cone; 5–500 hL capacity |
| L4.3.1.2: Glycol Jacket (Dimple Plate) | Dimple-welded or pillow-plate jacket on cone and sidewall for cooling/heating |
| L4.3.1.3: Pressure Relief & Spunding Valve | PRV + adjustable spunding valve for natural carbonation under pressure |
| L4.3.1.4: Temperature Probe & Thermowell | Pt100 or RTD in stainless thermowell; input to glycol PID controller |

#### L5.3.2: Yeast Management

Propagate, pitch, harvest, and store yeast. Healthy yeast at correct cell count (0.75–1.5 M cells/mL/°P) determines fermentation performance and flavour consistency.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.2.1: Yeast Propagation Tank | Aerated, temperature-controlled vessel for building yeast biomass from lab culture |
| L4.3.2.2: Yeast Dosing Pump | Sanitary positive-displacement pump for accurate pitching volume |
| L4.3.2.3: Yeast Storage Vessel | Chilled (2–4 °C) brink for harvested yeast between generations |

#### L5.3.3: Conditioning / Maturation Tank

Secondary maturation for flavour development, haze reduction, and natural carbonation. Lagers may condition 2–6 weeks at 0–2 °C (lagering). Some breweries use unitanks for combined fermentation + conditioning.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.3.1: Horizontal / Vertical Tank | Stainless vessel optimized for low-turbulence maturation |
| L4.3.3.2: Glycol Jacket | Cooling jacket for crash cooling and cold conditioning |
| L4.3.3.3: Carbonation Stone | Sintered stainless stone for CO2 injection during conditioning |

---

sections.title: L6.4 — Bright Beer Processing
sections.content:
Final preparation before packaging. Beer is filtered (unless hazy/unfiltered style), force-carbonated to target volumes of CO2, and held in pressure-rated bright tanks at 0–2 °C.

### L5 Systems under L6.4

#### L5.4.1: Beer Filtration

Removes yeast, haze-forming proteins, and particles. Not used for all styles (many craft ales are unfiltered). Technologies: plate-and-frame (DE), candle filter, cross-flow membrane.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.1.1: Plate & Frame / Candle Filter | DE (diatomaceous earth) or cellulose sheet filtration; 0.5–5 μm |
| L4.4.1.2: Cross-Flow Membrane Filter | Ceramic or polymer membrane; rising adoption in craft (no DE waste) |
| L4.4.1.3: Filter Aid Dosing & Recovery | DE slurry dosing pump and spent-DE handling |

#### L5.4.2: Carbonation System

Injects CO2 to achieve target carbonation (2.2–2.8 vol for most styles). Inline carbonation stone downstream of filter, upstream of bright tank.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.2.1: Inline Carbonation Stone | Sintered stainless stone or membrane diffuser in beer line |
| L4.4.2.2: CO2 Flow Controller | Mass flow controller dosing CO2 proportional to beer flow rate |
| L4.4.2.3: Dissolved CO2 Sensor | Inline optical or thermal conductivity sensor measuring carbonation level |

#### L5.4.3: Bright Beer Tank (BBT)

Pressure-rated holding tank (0.5–2.0 bar) for filtered, carbonated beer ready for packaging. Buffer between cellar and packaging line.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.3.1: Pressure-Rated Stainless Tank | 304/316L; rated to 2 bar; 10–500 hL |
| L4.4.3.2: Pressure & Level Sensors | Pressure transducer + capacitive or hydrostatic level sensor |
| L4.4.3.3: CIP Spray Ball | 360° coverage spray ball for automated cleaning |

---

sections.title: L6.5 — Packaging
sections.content:
Final step in the transformation chain. Beer is filled into consumer or trade containers. Packaging line selection depends on output type and volume. Oxygen pickup during packaging is the primary quality risk — dissolved oxygen target <50 ppb.

### L5 Systems under L6.5

#### L5.5.1: Bottle Filling Line

Rinse, counter-pressure fill, crown, and label glass bottles. Typical speeds: 20–600 bottles/min depending on brewery scale.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.1.1: Bottle Rinser / Washer | Water or air rinser removing dust and debris before filling |
| L4.5.1.2: Counter-Pressure Filler & Crowner | Isobaric filler minimizing CO2 loss; integrated crown applicator |
| L4.5.1.3: Labeler & Date Coder | Pressure-sensitive, glue, or shrink-sleeve labeler; inkjet date coder |

#### L5.5.2: Can Filling Line

Purge, fill, and seam aluminium cans. Fastest-growing packaging format in craft beer. Typical speeds: 30–2,000 cans/min. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.2.1: Can Purger & Rinser | CO2 purge and water rinse reducing dissolved oxygen pickup |
| L4.5.2.2: Can Filler | Gravity or counter-pressure filler; 4–80 fill heads |
| L4.5.2.3: Can Seamer | Double-seam closure head applying lid to filled can |

#### L5.5.3: Keg Filling Line

Wash, sanitize, fill, and seal stainless steel kegs (1/6 bbl, 1/4 bbl, 1/2 bbl). Keg washing is critical — returning kegs carry residual beer and potential contaminants.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.3.1: Keg Washer & Sanitizer | Multi-station automated washer: caustic → acid → rinse → CO2 purge |
| L4.5.3.2: Keg Filler | Counter-pressure filler maintaining carbonation; isobaric or volumetric |
| L4.5.3.3: Keg Capper & Spear Valve | Applies cap and checks spear valve integrity |

#### L5.5.4: Pasteurizer

Heat-treats packaged beer (62–72 °C for 15–30 s flash; or tunnel at 60 °C for 20 min) for shelf stability. Required for unpasteurized beer destined for warm-chain distribution; most craft draft skips this step.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.4.1: Tunnel / Flash Pasteurizer | Tunnel: water spray zones; Flash: plate heat exchanger before filler |
| L4.5.4.2: Temperature Controller | PID controller maintaining pasteurization temperature setpoint |
| L4.5.4.3: Pasteurization Unit (PU) Monitor | Calculates cumulative PU (time × temperature) for compliance verification |

---

sections.title: L6.6 — Glycol Cooling & Refrigeration (H)
sections.content:
Horizontal process running across L6.3 (Fermentation), L6.4 (Bright Beer), and L6.5 (Packaging cold rooms). A central glycol chiller circulates propylene glycol/water mixture (typically 30–35% glycol, −5 to +5 °C) through jacketed vessels and cold rooms. **This is where Marquardt's ultrasonic flow sensor provides the highest value** — glycol is a water/glycol mixture within the sensor's exact specification, and per-loop flow monitoring enables energy optimization and fault detection. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.6

#### L5.6.1: Glycol Chiller Unit

Central refrigeration package cooling the glycol/water reservoir. Compressor types: scroll (small craft), screw (mid-size), centrifugal (large). Sized to total cooling load (typically 5–500 kW). [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.1.1: Refrigeration Compressor | Scroll, screw, or reciprocating compressor; R-410A or R-449A refrigerant |
| L4.6.1.2: Condenser | Air-cooled or water-cooled condenser rejecting heat to ambient |
| L4.6.1.3: Evaporator / Plate Heat Exchanger | Brazed plate or shell-and-tube exchanger transferring cold to glycol loop |

#### L5.6.2: Glycol Distribution

Piping, pumps, and valves distributing glycol from chiller to all consuming vessels and back. Pipe sizing (typically DN20–DN50) must balance flow velocity and pressure drop.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.2.1: Glycol Circulation Pump | Centrifugal pump (cast iron or stainless); variable-speed for energy savings |
| L4.6.2.2: Distribution Manifold & Zone Valves | Header with solenoid or motorized valves per cooling zone (per fermenter, BBT, cold room) |
| L4.6.2.3: Glycol Piping & Insulation | Stainless or copper pipe with closed-cell foam insulation preventing condensation |

#### L5.6.3: Glycol Sensing & Control

Sensors monitoring glycol flow rate, temperature, and pressure at each cooling zone. Flow data enables per-fermenter energy accounting and early detection of jacket blockage or pump degradation. **MARQUARDT PRIMARY POSITION.** [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.3.1: Ultrasonic Flow Sensor | Transit-time ultrasonic measurement of glycol/water flow; no moving parts, zero pressure drop, glycol-tolerant. **MARQUARDT POSITION.** |
| L4.6.3.2: Paddle-Wheel / Turbine Flow Sensor | Mechanical flow sensor; incumbent in craft breweries; wears out in glycol service |
| L4.6.3.3: Temperature Probe (RTD / NTC) | Pt100 or NTC measuring glycol supply and return temperature per zone |
| L4.6.3.4: Pressure Transducer | Monitors glycol loop pressure for pump performance and leak detection |

#### L5.6.4: Glycol Reservoir

Insulated buffer tank holding glycol/water mixture. Provides thermal inertia and decouples chiller operation from instantaneous cooling demand.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.4.1: Reservoir Tank | Insulated stainless or polyethylene tank; 200–5,000 L |
| L4.6.4.2: Level Sensor & Makeup Dosing | Float or ultrasonic level sensor; glycol dosing to maintain concentration |
| L4.6.4.3: Glycol Concentration Monitor | Refractometer or density meter verifying glycol % (freeze protection) |

---

sections.title: L6.7 — Clean-In-Place & Sanitation (H)
sections.content:
Horizontal process running across all core L6 domains. CIP is non-negotiable in brewing — microbial contamination causes off-flavours, spoilage, and product loss. A typical 4-step cycle: pre-rinse → caustic wash (2–3% NaOH, 75–85 °C) → acid wash (1% phosphoric, 50–60 °C) → final rinse. All steps are flow-rate-dependent for effective cleaning. **Marquardt secondary position** — flow sensors verify CIP solution delivery at each circuit. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.7

#### L5.7.1: CIP Station / Skid

Centralized chemical storage, heating, and pumping unit. Typically 2–4 tanks (caustic, acid, rinse water, recovered rinse) with a supply and return pump. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.1.1: Caustic Tank & Heater | Insulated stainless tank (500–5,000 L) with electric or steam heating |
| L4.7.1.2: Acid Tank | Stainless tank for phosphoric or peracetic acid solution |
| L4.7.1.3: CIP Supply Pump | Sanitary centrifugal pump providing 2–4 bar, 50–200 L/min per circuit |

#### L5.7.2: CIP Distribution & Spray Devices

Piping, valves, and spray devices routing cleaning solution to every vessel, heat exchanger, and pipeline in the brewery.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.2.1: CIP Routing Valves | Pneumatic butterfly or mix-proof valves automating circuit selection |
| L4.7.2.2: CIP Return Pump | Returns spent solution to CIP station for reuse or drain |
| L4.7.2.3: Spray Balls & Rotary Jets | Static spray balls in tanks; rotary jet heads for large vessels (>50 hL) |

#### L5.7.3: CIP Sensing & Control

Sensors verifying that each CIP step meets time, temperature, flow rate, and chemical concentration targets. Flow rate verification ensures turbulent flow (Re > 30,000) for effective soil removal. **MARQUARDT SECONDARY POSITION.** [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.3.1: Flow Sensor | Verifies cleaning solution flow rate per circuit. Ultrasonic (Marquardt) or electromagnetic. **MARQUARDT SECONDARY.** |
| L4.7.3.2: Temperature Sensor | Pt100 monitoring CIP solution temperature at supply and return |
| L4.7.3.3: Conductivity Sensor | Measures chemical concentration (caustic vs rinse water) for step transitions |
| L4.7.3.4: CIP Controller / PLC | Automates sequence timing, valve actuation, and alarm generation |

---

sections.title: L6.8 — Water Treatment & Liquor Supply (H)
sections.content:
Horizontal process serving all core L6 domains. Brewing water ("liquor") represents ~90–95% of beer by weight. Water chemistry (Ca2+, Mg2+, SO42−, Cl−, alkalinity, pH) directly influences mash pH, hop character, and yeast health. A brewery uses 4–8 hL of water per hL of beer produced (including CIP and utilities). [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.8

#### L5.8.1: Water Treatment System

Filters and adjusts municipal or well water to target brewing profiles. Technologies: carbon filtration (chlorine removal), reverse osmosis (demineralization), UV sterilization, mineral dosing.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.8.1.1: Carbon Filter | Activated carbon removing chlorine and chloramine from municipal water |
| L4.8.1.2: Reverse Osmosis Membrane | RO system stripping >95% of dissolved minerals for base-water consistency |
| L4.8.1.3: Mineral Dosing & pH Adjustment | Chemical feed pumps adding CaCl2, CaSO4 (gypsum), lactic acid to target ion profile |

#### L5.8.2: Hot Liquor Tank (HLT)

Heated water storage (75–85 °C) for mashing and sparging. Receives pre-heated water recovered from wort cooling, reducing energy consumption. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.8.2.1: Insulated Vessel | Stainless vessel; 1–3× batch size; standing loss <2 °C/hr |
| L4.8.2.2: Heating Element / Steam Coil | Electric immersion heater (small) or steam coil (large) |
| L4.8.2.3: Temperature Controller & Sensor | PID controller with Pt100 maintaining setpoint ±1 °C |

#### L5.8.3: Cold Liquor Tank (CLT)

Chilled water storage (1–4 °C) for wort cooling via the plate heat exchanger. May be glycol-cooled or filled with pre-chilled municipal water overnight. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.8.3.1: Insulated Vessel | Stainless or polyethylene vessel with closed-cell insulation |
| L4.8.3.2: Cooling Coil / Glycol Jacket | Glycol-fed jacket or immersed coil maintaining 1–4 °C |
| L4.8.3.3: Temperature Controller & Sensor | PID controller ensuring cold liquor is at target before brew day |

#### L5.8.4: Water Flow Metering

Measures water intake (municipal meter), distribution to brewhouse, CIP, and packaging. Data drives water-use efficiency reporting (hL water / hL beer). **MARQUARDT TERTIARY POSITION** — water is the ideal medium for ultrasonic transit-time flow measurement.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.8.4.1: Ultrasonic Flow Sensor | Transit-time measurement on water intake and distribution lines. **MARQUARDT TERTIARY.** |
| L4.8.4.2: Electromagnetic Flow Meter | Mag-meter alternative; higher cost but handles higher conductivity variations |
| L4.8.4.3: Turbine Flow Meter | Mechanical incumbent; lower cost but moving parts wear in continuous service |

---

sections.title: L6.9 — Utilities, CO2 & Quality Assurance (H)
sections.content:
Combined horizontal domain covering steam/heat, compressed air, CO2 management, wastewater, and laboratory quality control.

### L5 Systems under L6.9

#### L5.9.1: Steam / Hot Water Generation

Provides process heat for brewhouse (kettle boil), CIP heating, and pasteurization. Natural gas fire-tube boilers dominate; electric boilers emerging in smaller craft facilities.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.9.1.1: Steam Boiler / Hot Water Generator | Fire-tube or water-tube boiler; 100–2,000 kW |
| L4.9.1.2: Condensate Return System | Recovers condensate for boiler feedwater; reduces water and energy consumption |
| L4.9.1.3: Steam Trap & Pressure Reducer | Maintains steam pressure at point of use; prevents water hammer |

#### L5.9.2: Compressed Air System

Plant air for pneumatic valve actuation, packaging equipment, and grain conveying. Sterile-filtered air for wort aeration.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.9.2.1: Air Compressor | Oil-free rotary screw or scroll compressor; food-grade air required |
| L4.9.2.2: Air Dryer & Sterile Filter | Refrigerated dryer + 0.01 μm sterile filter for process air |
| L4.9.2.3: Distribution Manifold | Header with regulators per consumer zone |

#### L5.9.3: CO2 Recovery & Distribution

Captures CO2 generated during fermentation, purifies, liquefies, and stores for reuse in carbonation, packaging purge, and tank blanketing. Reduces purchased CO2 costs by 50–80% in mid-to-large breweries.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.9.3.1: CO2 Scrubber & Purifier | Water scrubber + activated carbon removing volatile organics; food-grade CO2 output |
| L4.9.3.2: CO2 Compressor & Liquefier | Two-stage compressor + condenser liquefying CO2 for storage at −20 °C, 20 bar |
| L4.9.3.3: CO2 Storage Tank | Insulated pressure vessel (1–50 t) with level gauge and safety relief |

#### L5.9.4: Quality Assurance Laboratory

In-process and finished-product testing ensuring beer meets specifications. Tests: original gravity, alcohol by volume, pH, colour (SRM/EBC), bitterness (IBU), dissolved oxygen, microbiological stability.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.9.4.1: Density / Alcohol Analyzer | Digital density meter (Anton Paar) or distillation unit for ABV measurement |
| L4.9.4.2: pH & Dissolved Oxygen Meter | Benchtop or inline sensors for critical quality parameters |
| L4.9.4.3: Microbiological Testing Kit | Membrane filtration + incubator for spoilage organism detection |

#### L5.9.5: Wastewater Treatment

Brewery effluent (BOD 2,000–6,000 mg/L) must be treated before municipal discharge. On-site treatment reduces surcharges and environmental impact.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.9.5.1: pH Adjustment & Equalization Tank | Neutralizes acidic/caustic effluent (pH 2–12) to 6.5–8.5 range |
| L4.9.5.2: Aerobic / Anaerobic Digester | Reduces BOD/COD by 80–95%; anaerobic produces biogas for energy recovery |
| L4.9.5.3: Solids Separator | Dissolved air flotation (DAF) or settling tank removing suspended solids |

---

| L6.7 CIP (H) | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y |

| L6.9 Utilities/CO2/QA (H) | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y |

Notes: L6.6–L6.9 are universal horizontal — every output type requires cooling, cleaning, water, and utilities. Lager activates all core steps fully (including extended cold conditioning in L6.3 and filtration in L6.4). Hard seltzer/FMB may skip traditional mashing (sugar-wash base) — "partial" for L6.1/L6.2. Many craft ales and stouts skip filtration (L6.4 partial). Sour ales may add extended conditioning with Brettanomyces/Lactobacillus (months to years in L6.3).


**Primary L-path:** L7 > L6.6 Glycol Cooling & Refrigeration > L5.6.3 Glycol Sensing & Control > L4.6.3.1 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor

**Why this market:** Breweries operate water and glycol circuits in closed loops at 0–90 °C and <10 bar — exactly within Marquardt's operating envelope. Three positions offer cumulative volume per brewery:

| Position | Circuit | Medium | Why Marquardt Wins |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Glycol cooling loops | 30–35% propylene glycol/water | Glycol tolerance is a key differentiator; no-moving-parts avoids glycol-induced wear on turbine blades; glycol detection (FP extension) adds unique diagnostic value |
| **Secondary** | CIP return-flow verification | Water, caustic solution, acid solution | Zero pressure drop avoids flow restriction in CIP circuits; no moving parts eliminates contamination risk from sensor internals |
| **Tertiary** | Water intake & distribution | Municipal/treated water | Direct commodity match (flowmeter on water); KTW/WRAS approval enables potable-water circuits |

**Cost of failure:** Glycol flow sensor failure or inaccuracy leads to fermentation temperature excursions (±2 °C can produce off-flavours costing $5K–50K per batch in a mid-size brewery). CIP flow verification failure risks incomplete cleaning → contamination → spoiled batches. Water metering failure undermines efficiency reporting and regulatory compliance (water-use permits).

**Barriers:** (1) Food-grade material certifications (FDA 21 CFR 177, EC 1935/2004) — achievable but not yet held by Marquardt; (2) sanitary fittings (tri-clamp) for wort/beer-contact lines — only needed for CIP and water lines where standard threaded fittings are acceptable for glycol and utility water; (3) unfamiliar customer segment — brewery operations/engineering teams, especially craft, have different procurement patterns than HVAC OEMs. [SRC: 05_market_discovery]

rows.Name: Grain Handling & Milling
rows.Description: Receive, store, and crush malt and adjuncts into grist for mashing.

rows.Name: Brewhouse
rows.Description: Mash grist with hot water, separate wort, boil with hops, whirlpool, and cool — the core sugar extraction and flavour development sequence.

rows.Name: Fermentation & Conditioning
rows.Description: Pitch yeast into cooled wort; convert sugars to alcohol and CO2; mature beer to flavour target.

rows.Name: Bright Beer Processing
rows.Description: Filter, carbonate, and stabilize finished beer for packaging.

rows.Name: Packaging
rows.Description: Fill beer into bottles, cans, kegs, or bulk containers for distribution.

rows.Name: Water Treatment & Liquor Supply (H)

Direct commodity match (flowmeter on water); KTW/WRAS approval enables potable-water circuits
